findings.explanation: Neither the cluster nor a module variable exposes preferred_maintenance_window. Without it, AWS may apply engine patches and maintenance at any time, including business hours, and maintenance on Aurora clusters can trigger a brief failover. preferred_backup_window is already handled as a variable with a sensible off-peak default (04:00-09:00) elsewhere in this module — maintenance window has no equivalent.
findings.fix:
variable "preferred_maintenance_window" {
  type        = string
  description = "Preferred maintenance window"
  default     = "sun:03:00-sun:04:00"
}

# in aws_rds_cluster.default.tf:
  preferred_maintenance_window = var.preferred_maintenance_window
findings.remediation: Add a preferred_maintenance_window variable (e.g. default "sun:03:00-sun:04:00") and set it on aws_rds_cluster.default, following the same pattern already used for preferred_backup_window.
findings.title: aws_rds_cluster.default has no preferred_maintenance_window

findings.explanation: var.engine's validation block advertises support for aurora-mysql, aurora-postgresql, mysql, and postgres (variables.tf), but this resource hardcodes family = "aurora5.7" (an Aurora MySQL family) with no variable and no relationship to var.engine at all. Any consumer who sets engine to aurora-postgresql, mysql, or postgres gets a parameter group whose family doesn't match the cluster's actual engine, which AWS will reject at apply time. The resource's Terraform-local name ("examplea") and its name = "rds-cluster-pg" are also hardcoded, so two instantiations of this module in the same account/region collide on the parameter group name.
findings.remediation: Derive family from var.engine/var.engine_version (e.g. a lookup map from engine name to family prefix), and parameterize the parameter group name (or switch to name_prefix) so multiple module instances don't collide.
findings.title: aws_rds_cluster_parameter_group hardcodes family/name with no variables — breaks for 3 of the 4 supported engines

findings.explanation: aws_rds_cluster_instance.monitoring_interval is a number in the AWS provider schema, but the module declares var.monitoring_interval as type = string with default = 1 (a bare number Terraform silently coerces). This means the module's own type system doesn't actually constrain the input to numeric values the way it looks like it should — a non-numeric string passes the declared type check and only fails later, either in the validation regex or as an opaque provider error, instead of failing cleanly at the variable boundary.
findings.title: var.monitoring_interval is declared type = string but the AWS provider attribute is a number

findings.explanation: var.instances only requires length(var.instances) > 0 (variables.tf). Aurora's HA model depends on having at least one reader instance for the writer to fail over to; a caller who supplies exactly one instance (a legitimate-looking, valid config) gets a cluster with no failover target and a single point of failure. This is a judgment call rather than a hard defect — single-instance dev/test deployments are a reasonable use of this module — so it's flagged at low confidence rather than asserted as wrong.
findings.remediation: Consider documenting the HA implication of a single-instance var.instances in the variable's description, or adding a separate opt-in variable for environments that want the module to enforce >= 2 instances.
findings.title: Module places no lower bound on instance count, so callers can deploy a single-instance Aurora cluster with no failover target

findings.explanation: aws_backup_vault_lock_configuration.default sets min_retention_days = 35 but no changeable_for_days. Per the AWS provider, omitting changeable_for_days locks the vault immediately and permanently in Compliance mode with no grace period to correct a mistake — not even the root account can shorten or remove the lock once applied. HLD_AWS_202 (already satisfied here) only checks that a vault lock configuration exists, not that it has a grace period; holden's own remediation text for that policy actually recommends changeable_for_days = 3, but nothing enforces it.
findings.remediation: Set changeable_for_days (e.g. 3) on aws_backup_vault_lock_configuration.default so a misconfigured retention value can still be corrected within a short grace period before the lock becomes permanent.
findings.title: AWS Backup vault lock has no changeable_for_days grace period — becomes permanently unrecoverable immediately

findings.explanation: aws_backup_vault_lock_configuration.default.min_retention_days and aws_backup_plan.default's rule.lifecycle.delete_after are both hardcoded to the literal 35, duplicating var.backup_retention_period's default value (variables.tf) without referencing it. If a caller overrides backup_retention_period to a different value, native RDS automated-backup retention and the AWS Backup vault/plan retention silently diverge — exactly the kind of inconsistency a single source of truth variable is supposed to prevent.
findings.fix:
# vault lock: min_retention_days = var.backup_retention_period
# backup plan rule.lifecycle: delete_after = var.backup_retention_period
findings.remediation: Replace both hardcoded 35 literals with var.backup_retention_period so AWS Backup retention always tracks the same knob callers already use for native RDS backup retention.
findings.title: AWS Backup retention values are hardcoded to 35, duplicating var.backup_retention_period instead of referencing it

findings.explanation: aws_rds_cluster.default and both KMS keys in this module (aws_kms_key.aurora, aws_kms_key.flow_log in the example) all have lifecycle.prevent_destroy = true, but aws_backup_vault.default and aws_backup_plan.default — the resources whose entire purpose is disaster recovery — have none. An accidental terraform destroy, a module refactor that drops these blocks, or a state-manipulation mistake would silently delete the safety net meant to protect against exactly that kind of accident, with no Terraform-level guard rail.
findings.remediation: Add a lifecycle { prevent_destroy = true } block to both aws_backup_vault.default and aws_backup_plan.default, matching the protection already applied to the cluster and its KMS keys.
findings.title: AWS Backup vault and plan have no lifecycle.prevent_destroy, unlike every other critical resource in this module

findings.explanation: HOLDEN_BP_002 (fixed 2026-07-17) covered two distinct problems: the parameter group's family didn't match the engines var.engine claimed to support, and its name was a hardcoded literal that would collide across module instances. Only the family half was actually fixed — aws_rds_cluster_parameter_group.this still has name = "rds-cluster-pg" as a bare literal. Two callers of this module in the same account/region still get a DBParameterGroupAlreadyExists error on the second apply. Every other named resource this module creates (aws_backup_vault, aws_backup_plan, aws_iam_role.backup) already derives its name from var.cluster["cluster_identifier"] — the parameter group is the one holdout.
findings.remediation: Derive the name from var.cluster["cluster_identifier"], matching the naming convention already used for the backup vault/plan/IAM role in this module. Note: changing name forces replacement of the parameter group resource for anyone who has already applied this module, since name is ForceNew on aws_rds_cluster_parameter_group.
findings.title: Parameter group name is still a hardcoded literal, unresolved half of the earlier HOLDEN_BP_002 finding

suggested_policies.rationale: aws_rds_cluster has 9 existing security policies (encryption, access, backup, IAM) plus network-security proposals from a prior holden-ai-review pass (HLD_PROPOSED_001/002), but nothing checks preferred_maintenance_window. Confirmed to fire against this repo's aws_rds_cluster.default, which has no maintenance window set.
suggested_policies.yaml:
id: HLD_PROPOSED_003
name: RDS and Aurora clusters should specify a preferred maintenance window
principle: reliability
severity: LOW
rationale: |
  Without preferred_maintenance_window, AWS may apply engine patches and
  maintenance at any time of day, during peak business hours.
  Maintenance operations on RDS/Aurora clusters can trigger a failover or
  brief unavailability, so leaving the window unset trades a small amount
  of setup effort for unpredictable-timing outages.
remediation: |
  Set an explicit off-peak maintenance window:

    resource "aws_rds_cluster" "example" {
      preferred_maintenance_window = "sun:03:00-sun:04:00"
    }
resources:
  aws_rds_cluster:
    path: preferred_maintenance_window
    not_null: true

suggested_policies.rationale: HLD_AWS_202 already requires an aws_backup_vault to have a companion aws_backup_vault_lock_configuration, and its own remediation text recommends changeable_for_days = 3, but no policy actually checks that attribute is set. Confirmed to fire against this repo's aws_backup_vault_lock_configuration.default, which has min_retention_days set but no changeable_for_days.
suggested_policies.yaml:
id: HLD_PROPOSED_004
name: AWS Backup vault lock configurations should set a changeable_for_days grace period
principle: reliability
severity: MEDIUM
rationale: |
  Without changeable_for_days, a vault lock configuration becomes immediately
  and permanently enforced in Compliance mode — not even the root account can
  shorten retention or remove the lock afterward. A short grace period (e.g. 3
  days) preserves the ability to correct a misconfigured retention value before
  the commitment becomes irreversible, without meaningfully weakening the
  protection vault lock is meant to provide.
remediation: |
  Set an explicit grace period:

    resource "aws_backup_vault_lock_configuration" "example" {
      backup_vault_name   = aws_backup_vault.example.name
      changeable_for_days = 3
    }
resources:
  aws_backup_vault_lock_configuration:
    path: changeable_for_days
    not_null: true
